psychology.title: Psychological Framework
psychology.description: The Mechanics of the Escapist Mind

content.label: Avoidance Coping
content.text: Escapism acts as a 'buffer' against reality. In psychology, it is often a response to cognitive dissonance—the mental discomfort of living in a reality that doesn't match one's desires.

content.label: The Nostalgia Loop
content.text: The brain's Hippocampus often filters out negative historical data, leaving only 'Rosy Retrospection.' This explains why Charley views the 1890s as peaceful despite its actual hardships.

content.label: Neuro-Response
content.text: Chronic stress releases Cortisol. Escapist activities trigger the reward system to release Dopamine, creating a temporary 'level' of mental safety.

history.title: Grand Central Terminal
history.details: Grand Central is a 'liminal space'—a gateway between states of being. Its 1913 reconstruction created a labyrinthine depth that Jack Finney uses as a metaphor for the human subconscious. Just as the station has hidden tracks, the mind has hidden 'levels' of memory.

verdict.title: The Final Verdict
verdict.analysis: The Third Level is ultimately a Failure of Adaptation. While the 1890s appear calm, retreating into them is a form of 'Passive Regression.' A true solution requires Active Resilience—confronting the stressors of 2026 rather than hiding in a romanticized, non-existent past.
